NOT yet done (why level_current stops at 1, not higher): the other 5 decision classes are registered but not wired to any real call site; no approval interface exists to actually route a decision for review (that's A3); no sim-approver exists to draw realistic actual-effort data (that's A4); the FTE-required reporting surface Part 1b describes has no accruing data yet. 7 new tests (company/governance) + 3 new tests (simulation/renewals.py integration), epistemic PASS.

atoms.atom_name: Hedge-tariff alignment (cost locked when price is locked)
notes: 2026-07-10 DISCOVER finding, verified not assumed: MARGIN_REALISM Step 4's goal is substantially already built. Fixed/pass-through tariffs: decide_hedge_fraction() decided once per term (volatility as-of term start), held constant until the next renewal -- confirmed via real 2020 data (start_hf == avg_hf exactly for the large majority of customers). Deemed (out-of-contract/SVT-adjacent) tariffs: spot+premium, explicitly NO forward hedge -- correctly matches SVT tracking the cap's own wholesale-observation window. atoms.atom_name: Hidden adoption physics (bother-threshold, friction sensitivity, trust/reassurance, reward responsiveness)
notes: 2026-07-11 REGISTERED (P3, advisor-staged, director-decided -- ADOPTION_JOURNEY_REGISTER.md, do not start): ToU/control/DER adoption is a journey not a switch, and is the missing precondition for ToU tariffs meaning anything behaviourally. notes: 2026-07-12 AUTHORED AS PROPOSAL (EPOCH_GATING_AND_ATOM_AUTHORSHIP.md Rule 2, self-refill draw): a real, distinct epoch-5 gap named directly by the staged instruction's own worked example ('the regulatory/licence path'), confirmed absent from the map by direct read of every existing F/H-lane epoch-5 atom. H3_production_readiness_nfr_evidence/H4_go_live_nfr_register cover NON-FUNCTIONAL/operational readiness (SRE-style: availability, security, capacity); F3_obligations_register covers ONGOING per-transaction compliance invariants (already L3, built); F4_company_internal_authz covers INTERNAL authz/segregation of duties. None of the three model whether the simulated company could actually HOLD an Ofgem electricity/gas supply licence in the first place: the Standard Licence Conditions threshold tests (the fit-and-proper persons test), the post-2021-crisis financial resilience/ring-fencing requirements introduced after the Bulb/Avro/et al. supplier failures (customer credit-balance protection, minimum capital adequacy), and the Capacity Market/Renewables Obligation registration obligations that attach to holding a licence at all -- a real UK supplier's board treats this as a distinct go/no-go gate, decided by Ofgem's licensing function, not its own ops/security review. PROPOSAL ONLY -- not ranked, not built. Immediately workable in DISCOVER (read the real Ofgem licensing framework/Standard Licence Conditions text, the 2021-22 supplier-failure post-mortems that produced the financial-resilience reforms) per Rule 1; BUILD stays gated (loop_stage: idle) until the director ranks and promotes it, per Rule 2.

2026-07-12 RE-RANKED (director, RERANK_AND_PROVISIONAL_PLAN.md): ACCEPTED, 'DISCOVER now, build gated to Epoch 5' -- the licence path may CONSTRAIN Epoch-3 design, so discovering it late is expensive. DISCOVER done this pass (docs/market_research/ofgem_licence_readiness.md, real sourced findings via WebSearch/WebFetch against ofgem.gov.uk, not confabulated): three linked ongoing licence conditions SLC 4A (operational capability) / 4B (financial responsibility principle -- mutualised-cost management, the SoLR/RO/FiT socialisation mechanism) / 4C (ongoing fit-and-proper, SMR&I role-holders, introduced 2021); a REAL quantified Minimum Capital Requirement (Capital Floor GBP0, Capital Target GBP130/dual-fuel-equivalent domestic customer, decided 26 July 2023, phased via milestone assessments); CCB ring-fencing is a DIRECTED power (Ofgem may tell a supplier to ring-fence), not a blanket universal rule -- a company-specific regulatory state, not a constant; licence applications currently carry a 6-month processing timescale (Ofgem guidance updated 27 Feb 2026), confirming this is a live, current-day gate not a historical 2021-22 artefact only. level_current correctly stays 0 (DISCOVER does not move levels, MATURITY_MAP.md section 3). 2026-07-12 DISCOVER addendum, closes one named open item (self-refill draw, no BUILD code): SLC 4C's assessment criteria, previously flagged unresolved, now found and real. SLC 4C's fit-and-proper obligation is deliberately BROAD, not board/exec-only: it covers anyone with 'significant managerial responsibility or influence' (SMR&I) -- for a real supply business this explicitly includes heads of finance, risk, operations, technology, data, compliance, trading/hedging, strategy, and marketing/CRM, and can extend to external advisors or consultants with effective decision-making authority, not just directly-employed staff. Implication for this atom's own eventual framing: a real go/no-go SLC 4C assessment would need to model role-holder coverage across this WIDE set, not just a company's board -- a materially larger scope than 'are the directors fit and proper' alone. 2026-07-13 DISCOVER addendum, closes the milestone-assessment mechanics gap (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Real finding: milestone assessments are CUSTOMER-COUNT-triggered, not date-based -- a licensed supplier must pause new domestic customer onboarding on EACH relevant gas/electricity licence at the 50,000 and 200,000 customer thresholds, with an Ofgem assessment of operational capacity required before onboarding can resume. This is a concrete, real mechanism directly relevant to this project's own population-draw/growth atoms (W2_2) if this atom is ever built out: a real supplier's own growth trajectory is gated by regulatory review at named customer-count milestones, not smooth. A real Ofgem statement found ('a generalised shift... to direct debit has reduced the numbers of customers who fell into arrears') is exactly the kind of surface-level correlation this atom's own trap warns against over-reading as pure causation without accounting for the migration-driven selection effect. No BUILD code, no level change.

notes: director-confirmed known gap

2026-07-11 M3 DISCOVER+FRAME (docs/design/M3_DISCOVER_FRAME.md): population draw is genuinely greenfield -- CUSTOMERS is still a fixed list literal, _payment_rng is a single fixed seed (42+7919), zero draw_population/generate_population code found anywhere. Not currently a live epistemic violation (no seed exists yet for company code to read) but a real future risk if a draw module were placed carelessly (e.g. in saas/, which the portal already imports directly). BUILD deferred pending director/advisor sequencing.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER addendum (self-refill draw, no BUILD code): confirmed directly what the current fixed CUSTOMERS literal's real segment mix actually is (saas/customers.py) -- 15 resi / 4 SME / 5 I&C (24 total) -- the concrete marginal distribution any real per-run draw mechanism would need to preserve, not an abstract 'vary the population' instruction. External research grounds the mechanism choice in an established statistical tradition (population synthesis/microsimulation, not invented for this project): Iterative Proportional Fitting (IPF, established since 1940) is the standard technique for drawing a synthetic population matching known marginal distributions (e.g. this project's real segment/tariff proportions) while allowing genuine unit-level variation -- with known, real limitations directly relevant to sizing the eventual build: cloning-based IPF variants lose real heterogeneity (repeats existing customer shapes rather than generating genuinely new ones) and scale poorly as more attributes are matched jointly. A newer family (MCMC-based synthesis) is the alternative real practice where more attribute realism is needed. Named next FRAME question (not decided here): does this atom need IPF-grade marginal-matching rigour (defensible if segment/tariff proportions must exactly track the real book), or is a simpler weighted-bootstrap-with-replacement over the existing 24 real customer shapes sufficient for the stated goal ('vary the population run to run') -- a real sizing/scope call for the next FRAME pass, not decided unilaterally here.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass answers the named question above (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Read saas/customers.py directly rather than reasoning about IPF vs bootstrap in the abstract, and found a fact that reframes the whole question: CUSTOMERS is not a parametrically-generated table at all -- every one of the 24 entries is individually hand-authored with real narrative depth (named locations, specific tariff_type overrides for flex/pass-through mechanisms, specific write-off/vulnerability/life-event histories keyed to that exact customer_id). Confirmed via grep the real blast radius of touching this: 370 files under tests/company/saas/simulation reference specific customer_ids (C1, C3, C5, C_IC1, etc.) directly -- billing-tab tests, the year-spotlight dashboard's named-customer panels (phase-close checklist 0b requires a real named customer per phase), W2_5_life_event_stream's per-customer event stream, ledger write-off history, all keyed to these exact IDs surviving run to run. Neither IPF nor plain bootstrap-of-existing-rows was ever the right frame: IPF assumes an attribute-generation model to reweight, which doesn't exist here (there is no generator, only hand-written literals), and a full per-run REPLACEMENT population (new synthetic customer_ids each run) would break all 370 dependent references -- a real, wide blast radius, not a hypothetical one. FRAME decision: the eventual BUILD should be ADDITIVE, not REPLACIVE -- keep the existing 24 hand-authored customers as a fixed narrative core (preserving every downstream reference), and draw a per-run SYNTHETIC population on top of it (new customer_ids, e.g. G-prefixed or similar, generated from segment-proportion-matched attribute distributions derived from the real 24's own mix: 15/4/5 resi/SME/I&C) to deliver the atom's real_world_twin goal ('book composition varies run to run') without any one-way-door risk to already-built mechanisms. This also resolves the IPF-vs-bootstrap question as moot at this scale (N=24 core, small per-run addition) -- a straightforward weighted categorical draw over segment/tariff/region proportions is sufficient; IPF-grade joint-marginal matching would be over-engineering for a population this size. Next FRAME/BUILD question (not decided here, epoch-gated): what attribute distributions (consumption band, payment method, region) the synthetic add-on customers should draw from, and how many per run is realistic (a real supplier's book grows/shrinks by a small fraction per period, not a full reshuffle).

atoms.atom_name: Household budget sketch as hidden SIM state (income band, essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, savings buffer, priority-of-debts stack)
notes: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md item 1, director-decided). The core of the adjudicated finding: arrears are currently propensity-shaped; in reality they are the OUTPUT of a household budget meeting a price shock. This atom is that hidden SIM state -- income band (ONS-anchored income distributions/deciles), an essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, a savings buffer, and a debt stack with PRIORITY-OF-DEBTS ordering (rent/mortgage, council tax, food before energy -- anchored to debt-advice-sector priority-vs-non-priority-debt practice, e.g. StepChange/Citizens Advice guidance). Financial state joins the archetype reality the per-run population draw (W2_2) varies. WALL DISCIPLINE (record verbatim, AFFORDABILITY item 4): the SIM knows the budget; the company sees ONLY missed payments, broken plans, and what the customer discloses -- affordability is INFERRED through interfaces, never read. Anchors named, none encoded yet. Build in M3 with the population draw. LAW A/R12: never tune the budget distributions to hit a bad-debt number.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Income/margin anchored with real, current (2026) data: median UK household disposable income ~GBP3,225/month; critically, the aggregate 'GBP261/month left after essentials' figure masks real distributional structure -- the bottom 20% of earners face essential expenditure EXCEEDING income by GBP71/month, a genuinely NEGATIVE discretionary margin before any energy shock is applied, not merely a thin one. Essential-cost floor anchored directly and quantitatively: JRF/Trussell's Essentials Guarantee research gives GBP120/week (single adult) / GBP205/week (couple) for 2026/27; Universal Credit's April-2026 standard allowance (GBP98/GBP154) sits BELOW that floor by GBP22/GBP51 per week -- a real, policy-driven, structural shortfall for a material real UK population, independent of any energy price shock. IMPORTANT CORRECTION FOUND, not just confirmed (checked directly against Citizens Advice/StepChange rather than assumed): the atom's own registered debt-priority ordering ('rent/mortgage, council tax, food before energy') is NOT well supported -- real debt-advice-sector guidance classifies ENERGY AS a priority debt bracketed WITH rent/mortgage/council tax/HMRC (same tier, due to disconnection risk), not ranked below them; 'food' is not a debt-stack entry at all in this framework (no creditor relationship for future food purchases) and is better modelled as ongoing essential spending competing for the same discretionary pool. No precise within-priority-tier secondary ordering was found (a real, named open gap). Savings-buffer anchor not yet researched this pass (named gap, not skipped silently). No BUILD code, no level change. Full findings: docs/market_research/household_budget_w2_4.md.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER addendum, closes the savings-buffer gap named above (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Real, current FCA Financial Lives data, income-band-segmented: households earning under GBP15,000/year -- 22% have NO savings at all, over a third have less than GBP1,000. Households over GBP50,000/year -- only a small share lack savings, more than half hold over GBP10,000. Decisive finding: the savings buffer strongly CO-VARIES with income band -- should be modelled as a joint distribution, not two independently-drawn parameters. Population-wide baseline for sanity-checking: 1 in 10 UK adults have zero cash savings, a further 21% have less than GBP1,000. Additional real anchor: FCA estimates ~8.9 million UK adults in 'serious financial difficulty' (missed a bill payment in the last 6 months) -- connects this atom's hidden-budget-state concept directly to a real, sourceable population-scale calibration target. All five named components (income band, essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, savings buffer, priority-of-debts) are now real-anchored; only the within-priority-tier secondary ordering remains genuinely open. No BUILD code, no level change.

2026-07-13 SECOND SEARCH on the within-priority-tier ordering, still genuinely unresolved (honest, not abandoned). A second targeted search surfaced a real terminology tension worth registering, not resolving by picking whichever framing is convenient: some casual/summary sources use 'priority bills' loosely to mean 'food, energy, rent, council tax' together (essential must-pay COSTS), while the precise StepChange/Citizens Advice DEBT framework (already found) distinguishes formal priority DEBTS (a creditor relationship: rent, council tax, energy, HMRC) from ongoing SPENDING like food (no creditor relationship for a future purchase) -- both framings are real, from real sources, but answer different questions; this atom's own debt-stack concept should use the precise (debt-framework) sense, not the casual one, per the correction already registered above. No source found gives a strict tie-breaking order WITHIN the priority-debt tier (rent vs energy vs council tax when money is insufficient for all three) after two real, targeted search attempts -- confirmed genuinely un-anchorable via this method, not merely unsearched. notes: 2026-07-11 REGISTERED (EPOCH2_RATIFIED.md immediate effect 1): director-ratified NEW W-lane, three amendments to THE_VALUE_CYCLE_FRAMING.md (banking-as-swappable-interface worry). Built adapter-SHAPED behind an interface mirroring the real protocol -- submission windows, multi-day cycle, AUDDIS-style mandate setup, ARUDD-style failure returns with reason codes, ADDACS amendments; cash lands when the rails say, making the banked clock (D2_three_clocks/C2) honest -- 'rails ARE late-arriving truth, a limb of the spine, not an adjacent system.' Full typed/versioned hardening of this seam is Epoch-3 work (added to the wall's adapter inventory). loop_stage=idle: M2 build (this atom included) starts only after the M2 entry-gate payments-maturity-audit verdict AND M1's exit test both land -- not before, per EPOCH2_RATIFIED.md immediate effect 4.

2026-07-11 'RAILS SIM FIRST' BUILT, level 0->1 (advisor steer ADVISOR_STEER_M2_OPEN.md: 'audit reporting unlocks M2 BUILD... Open M2 build: rails sim first'). WebSearch-verified real Bacs mechanics before building, not assumed: 3-working-day processing cycle (Pay.UK's own Bacs System Principles guide); AUDDIS confirms/rejects mandate setup on Day 2; ARUDD reports a failed collection up to 2 working days AFTER collection day, never instantly (the actual 'cash lands when the rails say' physics); real reason codes (ARUDD 0='Refer to Payer' -- the real dominant insufficient-funds-class code -- 1/2/3; AUDDIS 1/2/3/5), sourced from AccessPaySuite/Hafiz Didarali Bacs reason-code references. Deliberately does NOT duplicate the existing calibrated behavioural failure-probability model (simulation/arrears_engine.py::payment_outcome(), stress-tier-anchored, R13-compliant) -- this module only adds realistic TIMING and REASON CODES around an already-decided success/failed outcome, structurally guarded by a test proving the module contains no probability constants of its own. Reason-code selection is deterministic to the documented dominant code, NOT rng-randomised across the full code set -- no sourced real-world frequency split between codes exists, and fabricating one would make rare codes (Payer Deceased) appear unrealistically often. NOT yet done: wiring this into the live company/billing/direct_debit.py collection flow (still synchronous/instant today) -- a separate, larger integration step, per the framing's own sequencing ('rails sim first, THEN estimated billing -> catch-up rebilling -> settlement timetable -> DD cash engine'). 21 new tests, epistemic PASS (pure stdlib module, zero company/saas coupling).

2026-07-11 RAILS TIMING WIRED INTO THE LIVE DD FLOW, level 1->2 (director in-console approval: 'M2 next step approved: wire rails timing into the live DD flow'). Real finding before building (R4): grepped the whole codebase for company/billing/direct_debit.py's DirectDebitBook/record_attempt/DDPaymentAttempt and found ZERO callers anywhere -- entirely unwired, the same 'paper compliance' class the M2 audit already named for 18 OTHER billing/collections modules. The genuinely live DD-relevant flow is simulation/arrears_engine.py::payment_outcome(), called from compute_emergent_bad_debt()/compute_debt_recovery() -- but those compute REAL ground-truth bad-debt/recovery figures flowing into net_margin_gbp/treasury_cash_balance_gbp, exactly the class of code M1's own precedent already established extreme caution around; a same-day timing change there would shift which calendar year a write-off lands in, silently altering historical financial figures. Deliberately NOT touched. Instead built new simulation/dd_collection_book.py: populates the previously-unwired DirectDebitBook with REAL Bacs-timed collection records, using the SAME unchanged payment_outcome() decision (identical RNG seed and call sequence as compute_emergent_bad_debt()) layered with bacs_rails.py's realistic timing/reason codes -- a new, real, company-observable artefact where none existed before, zero existing numbers changed (confirmed via git status: arrears_engine.py and direct_debit.py both untouched, import-only integration). Caught and fixed a real bug before it shipped: initially shared one rng between payment_outcome() and resolve_submission()'s lag-day draw, which would have desynced every outcome after the first resolved bill from the real ground truth -- fixed with a separate, independently-seeded rng for the rails-timing layer, proven by a dedicated regression test replaying the exact same payment_outcome() sequence independently and asserting an exact match. 9 new tests, epistemic PASS, full arrears_engine test suite (74 tests) still green.

2026-07-12 EXPERT HOUR RUN, verdict NEEDS_WORK (fresh-context phase-close-evaluator, no memory of the build) -- an attempted L2->L3 build (mandate SETUP/AMENDMENT wired through the same rails-timing pattern as collections) did NOT earn L3, correctly held at level 2. Concrete findings, all from reading the actual code: (1) DECISIVE -- simulation/dd_collection_book.py has ZERO callers from any real run pipeline (confirmed by grep), only exercised by its own tests -- fails 'lives in time' (L3's own bar), phase-close rule 0b (evidence on a business surface), and R1 (consumer-verified); this alone caps the atom below L3 regardless of the wiring's own quality. Registered as the next real step, not attempted same-pass (a live-pipeline integration this late in a session, into a ~500s historical-replay run, was judged too risky to rush). (2) FIXED: the amendment trigger originally compared a single bill's raw amount against the mandate's stored amount, firing on almost every seasonal swing -- modelling on-demand billing, not smoothed Variable DD. Now compares against a rolling MEDIAN (not mean -- a mean is dragged by a single outlier, a median correctly ignores one anomalous bill while still moving once a majority of a trailing 12-bill window reflects a genuine sustained change) over a trailing window, and amends TO the median, never to the raw triggering bill. (3) FIXED: the original 'no calibrated mandate-rejection rate exists to anchor a failure model' claim was factually wrong -- this lane's own charter cites GoCardless's public mandate-lifecycle data (~95% confirmed by day 5, i.e. a real ~5% non-confirmation rate is citable). Outcome remains deterministic success in this pass, but the honest limiting factor is corrected: no fallback-payment-method/retry mechanism exists anywhere in this codebase for a rejected mandate, not 'no rate exists'. (4) FIXED: an earlier version of this build's own docstring claimed the M2 audit's duplicated-register finding (DirectDebitBook vs company/billing/dd_mandate_register.py) was 'resolved'/'superseded' -- it was not; only a docstring changed, both classes remain instantiable, nothing structurally prevents a second writer. dd_mandate_register.py's own docstring corrected to state this honestly as an OPEN, unresolved duplication (R10) rather than a closed one -- also fixed a self-contradiction in the original wording (it declared DirectDebitBook canonical while admitting dd_mandate_register.py has the cleaner as_of/point-in-time discipline, this project's own primary architectural law). (5) STILL OPEN, honestly registered rather than silently implied fine: mandate setup is submitted and resolved in the same step as the collection it precedes, rather than genuinely gating the collection on AUDDIS confirmation first -- fixing this properly would delay a new DD customer's first collection date, risking a change to ground-truth arrears/bad-debt figures already baked into compute_emergent_bad_debt(), the same class of change this atom's build has consistently avoided. (6) Test suite strengthened per the review's own request: a ground-truth-replay test now covers the amendment-firing code path with varying amounts (the pre-existing regression tests only used a constant amount and never exercised it); the 'setup wiring does not change collection outcome' test now compares against an independently-computed real baseline instead of a loose bound. 6 new/changed tests, epistemic PASS. level_current correctly stays at 2 -- the fixes are real and worth keeping, but do not themselves close the two structural L3 blockers (pipeline wiring, genuine register consolidation).

2026-07-12 SECOND EXPERT HOUR REVIEW, narrow finding fixed same session: a real product-mechanic incoherence in the rendered surface itself (phase-close rule 0c, reading one instance as a human) -- the mandate's own smoothed/re-estimated monthly_amount_gbp read as if it were a Fixed-DD instalment amount, directly above a collection table where every real attempt was the raw, varying bill total, with no code path connecting the two. This corresponds to no real UK DD product: a genuine smoothed Fixed DD collects the fixed amount and tracks seasonal variation as a running balance; a genuine Variable DD collects the real bill each cycle with no fixed mandate figure gating anything. The code always collected the real bill amount (submit_collection() uses the bill's own total_amount_gbp, never mandate.monthly_amount_gbp) -- i.e. this always WAS Variable DD -- but neither the docstring nor the site surface said so. FIXED BY LABELLING, deliberately not re-engineering (the reviewer's own specified remedy option (b)): site/supplier/index.html's copy and portfolio KPI now state explicitly this is Variable DD, the mandate figure is an 'estimated reference level' that never sizes or gates a collection (only decides when an ADDACS re-estimation notice fires), and the KPI is relabelled 'Est. Reference Total' away from the misleading 'Monthly Collection'. No change to which cash amount is actually collected -- ground truth stays exactly as compute_emergent_bad_debt() computed it. This product-choice registration is the entry itself (R10: nothing may be simplified silently).

2026-07-12 REGISTER-CONSOLIDATION BLOCKER, investigated and partially closed: the second Expert Hour review suggested a future migration should move DirectDebitBook's callers onto company/billing/dd_mandate_register.py::DDMandateRegister, given its cleaner as_of point-in-time discipline. Investigated directly rather than deferred again: that direction is not a clean migration -- DDMandateRegister has NO attempt-tracking concept at all (its own docstring: 'this module tracks the mandate, not individual payments'), so migrating onto it would still need a second, bolted-on structure for collection attempts, not actually eliminating the duplication. FIXED instead, the other direction: DirectDebitMandate gained last_status_change_date, and cancel_mandate()/reinstate_mandate() gained optional as_of parameters that stamp it -- folding DDMandateRegister's real advantage into the module that already has a live caller and already tracks attempts, additive and backward-compatible (no as_of passed = identical behaviour to before). dd_mandate_register.py's own docstring corrected with this finding and a go-forward retirement recommendation (not carried out this pass -- deleting real historical work is a bigger, more consequential step than enriching the live module, left as an explicit future action). The duplication is NOT fully resolved (dd_mandate_register.py itself is not deleted, still exists, still unused) but the underlying REASON it mattered -- DirectDebitBook lacking a real point-in-time discipline -- is now closed. 6 new tests, epistemic PASS.

2026-07-12 THIRD PASS: the last named L3 blocker (mandate setup not genuinely gating the collection it precedes) fixed. The prior reasoning for leaving this open twice -- 'risks changing ground-truth arrears/bad-debt outcomes' -- was re-examined precisely rather than re-asserted, and found to conflate two independent things: payment_outcome()'s success/fail decision (compute_emergent_bad_debt()'s own ground truth) is drawn from the shared rng BEFORE any date logic runs and takes no date as an input at all, so gating a collection's own due_date can never change which bills succeed or fail; and this module's own dates feed nothing but a business-surface rendering (extract_dd_rails()), never the ledger/cash-timing pipeline that computes any actually-published financial figure. FIXED: a brand-new mandate's first collection due_date is now max(bill's own due_date, mandate's own AUDDIS confirmation date) -- genuinely gated, matching what a real Bacs integration requires. Verified against the REAL full pipeline run (not just unit tests, ~580s, all 3 real end-to-end tests still pass) with this change in place, in addition to 3 new/rewritten unit tests covering the gate itself, the outcome-independence safety property, and that an already-established mandate's second+ collection is NOT further gated. All named L3 blockers from both prior Expert Hour reviews are now addressed: pipeline wiring (closed pass 2), product-mechanic labelling (closed pass 2), register consolidation (closed pass 3, prior entry), and mandate-setup gating (closed this entry). Next real step: a fresh, non-self-graded Expert Hour review of the FULL current state before any level claim.
